parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the injected `dir` parameter to pivot from read-only listing to database tampering.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may alter the query or execute stacked SQL if the driver allows it.
parsed_findings.proof: `dir=asc;DROP TABLE users--` is appended into the ORDER BY clause unescaped.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `?sort=name&dir=asc;DROP TABLE users--` and inspect the generated query.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `direction` is interpolated directly into SQL, enabling injection through `dir`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass the broken filter, then use the proxy to reach otherwise inaccessible internal URLs.

parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:80/` or a metadata IP and observe the server makes the request.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side, creating an SSRF primitive against internal or cloud endpoints.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Start with `../../` in `file`; the first `../` is removed, leaving a still-traversing path.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read arbitrary server files reachable by the process.
parsed_findings.proof: `../../etc/passwd` becomes `../etc/passwd`, and `path.join('/srv/data', f)` resolves outside the base dir.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: GET `/download?file=../../etc/passwd` and the handler reads a file outside `/srv/data`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Single `replace('../','')` only strips one traversal token, so `../../` still escapes `/srv/data`.

parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution or full server compromise if an attacker can send a cookie.
parsed_findings.proof: A crafted `state` cookie can contain a base64-encoded pickle payload that runs during `pickle.loads(...)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set `state` to a base64 pickle gadget and request `GET /resume`; the payload executes before `last_page` is read.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `pickle.loads` deserializes attacker-controlled cookie data, enabling arbitrary code execution.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the IDOR to target a victim record, then modify it through req.body.
parsed_findings.impact: Account tampering or takeover if sensitive fields are writable.
parsed_findings.proof: findById(req.params.id) is saved without any ownership or role check.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH /api/users/<victimId> with any JSON body as a low-privilege user.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Any authenticated user can update any account by supplying another user's :id.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: After reaching a user record, overwrite privileged attributes through the body.
parsed_findings.impact: Privilege escalation, password reset, or invariant corruption.
parsed_findings.proof: Every key in req.body is merged into the persisted record before save().
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send role,isAdmin,passwordHash,or similar fields in the PATCH body.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Object.assign copies attacker-controlled fields into the user model without filtering.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send a crafted `name` value to the endpoint.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution as the app user.
parsed_findings.proof: `name='a;id'` makes `os.system` run `tar` then `id`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/backup` with JSON `{"name":"a;id","src":"/var/data/app"}`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `name` is interpolated into a shell command, enabling command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use a payload after `/var/data/` and trigger the backup.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution through the backup endpoint.
parsed_findings.proof: `src='/var/data/x;id'` passes `startswith` and executes `id` in the shell.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/backup` with JSON `{"src":"/var/data/x;id"}`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `src` prefix check is bypassable because `src` is also passed to `os.system` unquoted.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Submit malicious markup, store it through `create_comment`, and trigger execution when `list_comments` serves the saved HTML.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can run arbitrary JavaScript in other users' browsers when comments are viewed.
parsed_findings.proof: `markdown.markdown(body)` can preserve raw HTML, and the result is saved then returned by `/comments`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `<script>alert(1)</script>` as `body`, then load comments to see the script in stored HTML.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User markdown is rendered without sanitization and stored as HTML, enabling stored XSS.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send a negative amount to bypass the insufficient-funds check.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can create money or steal value by sending negative transfers.
parsed_findings.proof: `amt < 0` makes line 6 pass, line 8 adds to the sender, and line 9 subtracts from the recipient.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `{"to":"victim","amount":-100}` to increase the sender balance by 100.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Negative `amount` is accepted and turns a transfer into a balance mint or drain.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Race the balance read against the later writes in separate requests.
parsed_findings.impact: Concurrent requests can double-spend or lose updates, corrupting account balances.
parsed_findings.proof: Two requests can both read the same `src` at line 5 before either update at lines 8-9.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Fire two transfers at once from one account whose balance only covers one of them.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The balance check and updates are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can overspend the same funds.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Forge a JWT payload, 2) make verification fail, 3) production path returns the unsigned claims.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can impersonate arbitrary users and bypass authentication.
parsed_findings.proof: `jwt.decode()` does not check the signature, and this branch returns its payload when verification throws.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send any JWT with an invalid signature while `NODE_ENV=production`; the function returns the decoded claims.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Production falls back to `jwt.decode(t)` after a failed verify, so any forged token is accepted.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: `login_required` only proves authentication; the SQL query then returns the requested user row directly.
parsed_findings.impact: This is an IDOR that leaks other users' account data and enables user enumeration.
parsed_findings.proof: An authenticated user can request `/u?id=2` and receive `id,email` for user 2 instead of only their own record.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Log in as user A, call `/u?id=<victim_id>`, and observe the victim's email in the response.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: This endpoint exposes any user's email to any logged-in caller by trusting the `id` parameter without ownership checks.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the timing oracle to recover the full hex digest, then replay the request with the forged header.
parsed_findings.impact: A forged signature can bypass webhook authentication and trigger `process_event` without knowing `SECRET`.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can measure response-time differences while guessing `X-Sig` prefixes against the same body.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many requests with the same body and varying `X-Sig` guesses, then refine the prefix from faster responses.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The HMAC is compared with `==`, which can leak timing differences and enable signature forgery.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass the filter with `//host`, then use the redirect in a phishing or OAuth token theft flow.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can send users to phishing or malware pages while appearing to come from this site.
parsed_findings.proof: `nxt.startswith('/')` passes for `//attacker.tld`, then `redirect(nxt)` issues a browser-followed external redirect.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/go?next=//evil.com` and observe a redirect to `evil.com`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The path-only check accepts protocol-relative URLs, so `//evil.com` is treated as valid and redirected externally.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use injected query operators to make the lookup return any matching user, then complete the login flow.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can bypass authentication and log in as another user.
parsed_findings.proof: Sending JSON like `{"email":{"$ne":null},"password":{"$ne":null}}` makes `findOne(q)` match unintended accounts.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/login` with JSON operator objects in `email` and `password` instead of strings.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-supplied `email` and `password` are embedded directly into a MongoDB query, enabling NoSQL operator injection.

parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can crash the process or potentially hijack control flow.
parsed_findings.proof: `gets` has no length check, so any input over 63 bytes plus NUL writes past `buf`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run the program and paste more than 64 bytes at stdin.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer and can overflow it.

parsed_findings.impact: An attacker who can access app data can steal the token and reuse the session.
parsed_findings.proof: `putString('access_token', token)` writes the token to app storage in plaintext.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Save a token, then read the app's shared_prefs XML or backup data and recover it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The access token is stored in SharedPreferences without encryption.

parsed_findings.impact: Confidential data encrypted with this function is exposed if the code leaks or is shared.
parsed_findings.proof: KEY is a fixed byte string literal instead of being loaded from a secret store or derived per deployment.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Read the source and reuse the constant key to decrypt any `enc()` output.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone with the code can decrypt all ciphertexts and reuse the key.

parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can modify protected data or mount padding-oracle style attacks if a decrypt path exists.
parsed_findings.proof: The function returns `iv + ciphertext` only; there is no MAC or AEAD tag to verify integrity.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Flip bits in a ciphertext block and observe the corresponding plaintext change after decryption.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: CBC encryption is unauthenticated, so attackers can tamper with ciphertext and alter decrypted plaintext undetected.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pass a NULL output pointer with a positive size to bypass the check and hit the dereference.
parsed_findings.impact: A caller can trigger a null-pointer crash, causing denial of service.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 3 returns only when `out` is non-NULL, so the unsafe path accepts `out == NULL` and then writes through it.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("A", NULL, 1)` and it will crash on the copy or terminator write.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The guard is inverted, so a NULL `out` with `out_sz>0` reaches `memcpy` and `out[n]` and dereferences it.
